title: Prolonged Fever with Abdominal Discomfort
subtitle: Medicine Ward · Day 3 of Admission

clinicalPresentation.chiefComplaint: Stepwise rising fever for 10 days with headache, abdominal discomfort and loose stools

HOPI.onset: Insidious onset low-grade fever starting 10 days ago, gradually rising in a stepwise pattern
HOPI.progression: Fever now reaching 39.5°C with predominantly evening spikes. Frontal headache and generalised malaise throughout. Loose stools (3–4/day, no blood) and abdominal distension appeared on day 5.
associatedSymptoms: One episode of epistaxis on day 8

familyHistory: No similar illness in hostel mates
personalHistory: Eats from hostel canteen and roadside stalls regularly

others: Rose spots: 2–3 faint pink macules on upper abdomen (easily missed)
CVS: Relative bradycardia — pulse 78 despite temperature 39.2°C (Faget's sign)
Chest: Clear with occasional dry cough

clinicalClue: KEY FINDING: Relative bradycardia (pulse inappropriately low for the degree of fever). Most infections cause tachycardia — this is the opposite. Also note the rose spots on the abdomen.

effects.penaltyMsg: Untreated typhoid progressing to intestinal phase. Complication risk is now rising.
Typhoid perforation or haemorrhage has occurred. Emergency surgical management required.
Patient died from complications of untreated typhoid.

results.first_week: WBC: 3,800/μL (LEUCOPENIA). Lymphocyte predominance. Hb 10.8 g/dL (mild anaemia). Platelets: 140k (mildly low).
results.second_week: WBC: 3,200/μL (leucopenia persisting). Hb 9.4 g/dL (worsening). Platelets 98k (thrombocytopenia — watch carefully).
results.complicated: WBC: 18,000/μL (LEUCOCYTOSIS — NEW FINDING — suggests perforation/superinfection). Hb 7.2. Platelets 62k. Band forms present.

interpretation.first_week: CRITICAL CLUE: Leucopenia in a febrile patient is the opposite of most infections. Classic for typhoid. Most bacterial infections cause leucocytosis.
interpretation.second_week: Persistent leucopenia with worsening anaemia and thrombocytopenia — typhoid course progressing.
interpretation.complicated: Sudden leucocytosis in established typhoid = perforation or secondary infection. Surgical emergency.

results.first_week: No malaria parasites seen. RBC morphology normal. NEGATIVE for P. falciparum and P. vivax.

interpretation.first_week: Malaria ruled out — critical differential in febrile patient from Nepal. Typhoid remains top differential.

tests.name: Widal Test
tests.fullName: Widal Test (O and H Agglutination Titres)

results.first_week: TO: 1:80 (borderline). TH: 1:80. NOTE: Widal may be false-negative in first week — antibodies take time to develop. Single titre not diagnostic.
results.second_week: TO: 1:320 (positive). TH: 1:160 (positive). Rising titre strongly supports typhoid.
results.complicated: TO: 1:640 (very high). TH: 1:320. Confirmed enteric fever.

interpretation.first_week: Low Widal titre in first week does NOT exclude typhoid. Blood culture is more useful at this stage.
interpretation.second_week: Titre of 1:320 in endemic area is highly suggestive of typhoid. Treat accordingly.
interpretation.complicated: Very high titre confirms established typhoid. Note: antibiotics may blunt titre rise.

results.first_week: POSITIVE: Salmonella typhi isolated (72h incubation). Sensitive to: Ceftriaxone, Azithromycin. RESISTANT to: Ampicillin, Chloramphenicol (MDR strain).
results.second_week: POSITIVE: Salmonella typhi isolated. Same sensitivity pattern. Note: positivity drops to ~50% in 2nd week.
results.complicated: POSITIVE: Salmonella typhi + E. coli (polymicrobial — from perforation). Broad spectrum cover required.

interpretation.first_week: Gold standard — positive in 80% of first-week cases. Confirms typhoid AND guides antibiotic choice (MDR strain present).
interpretation.second_week: Still useful but lower sensitivity. Confirms typhoid and antibiotic sensitivities.
interpretation.complicated: Polymicrobial growth indicates bowel perforation with faecal contamination.

results.first_week: Bilirubin: 1.8 mg/dL (mildly elevated). AST: 78 U/L (2x upper limit). ALT: 65 U/L (elevated). ALP: Normal. Pattern of typhoid hepatitis.
results.second_week: Bilirubin: 2.4 mg/dL. AST: 120 U/L. ALT: 98 U/L. Hepatitis picture worsening.
results.complicated: Bilirubin: 4.2 mg/dL (clinical jaundice). PT prolonged. Coagulopathy developing.

interpretation.first_week: Mild transaminitis is a helpful diagnostic clue — typhoid hepatitis occurs in 20–40% of cases.
interpretation.second_week: Worsening hepatic involvement. Monitor closely for acute liver failure.
interpretation.complicated: Severe hepatic dysfunction with coagulopathy significantly increases surgical risk.

results.first_week: NS1 Antigen: NEGATIVE. IgM: NEGATIVE. IgG: NEGATIVE. Dengue excluded.

interpretation.first_week: Dengue excluded — important differential for fever + thrombocytopenia in endemic region.
interpretation.second_week: Dengue ruled out. Focus remains on typhoid.

results.first_week: Hepatomegaly (14.5 cm). Mild splenomegaly (12 cm). Mesenteric lymphadenopathy — multiple enlarged nodes at RIF. No free fluid.
results.second_week: Hepatosplenomegaly increased. Thickened bowel wall at terminal ileum. Small mesenteric nodes. No free fluid yet.
results.complicated: Free fluid in peritoneum (ascites). Dilated bowel loops. Possible perforation at terminal ileum. Emergency CT/surgery.

interpretation.first_week: Classic USG triad: hepatomegaly + splenomegaly + mesenteric lymphadenopathy = highly suggestive of typhoid.
interpretation.second_week: Terminal ileal wall thickening — Peyer's patch involvement is pathognomonic for typhoid.
interpretation.complicated: Free fluid strongly suggests intestinal perforation. Surgical emergency.

results.first_week: Processing (72h). Low positivity in week 1. Preliminary: No Salmonella seen on direct smear.
results.second_week: POSITIVE: Salmonella typhi isolated in stool. Sensitive to Ceftriaxone, Azithromycin.
results.complicated: POSITIVE: Salmonella typhi + E. coli — mixed bowel flora, consistent with perforation.

interpretation.first_week: Blood culture is more useful than stool culture in first week — Salmonella shedding in stool peaks in weeks 2–3.
interpretation.second_week: Positive stool culture in 2nd week confirms diagnosis. Also useful for contact tracing.

results.first_week: Mildly cloudy. WBC: Trace. Protein: Trace (non-specific). Glucose: Absent. No bacteria. Not consistent with UTI.
results.second_week: Similar findings. Urine culture: Salmonella typhi isolated (Salmonella shed in urine in ~25% of cases).
results.complicated: Oliguria — urine output <0.5 mL/kg/hr. Reflects pre-renal AKI from septic shock.

interpretation.first_week: UTI excluded. Trace abnormalities are non-specific.
interpretation.second_week: Salmonella in urine is a secondary diagnostic clue.
interpretation.complicated: Oliguria signals inadequate renal perfusion — septic shock.

results.first_week: Clear lung fields. No consolidation, no effusion. Heart size normal. Unremarkable.
results.second_week: Mild basal atelectasis (non-specific, from abdominal distension). No pneumonia.
results.complicated: Free gas under right dome of diaphragm — PNEUMOPERITONEUM. Confirms bowel perforation. Surgical emergency.

interpretation.first_week: Normal CXR. Pneumonia excluded. Not useful for diagnosing typhoid.
interpretation.second_week: Atelectasis from abdominal distension. Non-specific finding.
interpretation.complicated: Free air under diaphragm = perforation confirmed. Emergency laparotomy required.

first_week.note: Oral rehydration preferred if tolerating. IV for persistent fever and poor intake.

second_week.note: IV fluids important — diarrhoea and poor oral intake leading to dehydration.

complicated.note: Aggressive IV resuscitation for septic shock.

first_week.note: Correct — symptomatic relief for fever. Does not treat the infection.

second_week.note: Continue antipyretic. Avoid NSAIDs — risk of GI bleeding in typhoid.

complicated.note: Paracetamol for fever. NSAIDs completely contraindicated in complicated typhoid.

first_week.note: Caution — NSAIDs increase risk of GI bleeding in typhoid. Use paracetamol instead.

second_week.note: DANGEROUS — NSAIDs contraindicated in typhoid. High risk of intestinal haemorrhage.

complicated.note: CRITICAL ERROR — NSAIDs in complicated typhoid causes catastrophic GI bleeding.

first_week.note: Monitoring initiated. Watch for sudden abdominal pain or melaena.

second_week.note: Careful watch for perforation signs — sudden pain, peritonism, or melaena.

complicated.note: ICU monitoring. Hourly urine output, serial abdominal examinations.

first_week.note: Excellent choice! Ceftriaxone is first-line for typhoid. Fever will begin settling in 3–5 days. Patient recovers fully.

second_week.note: Ceftriaxone started — effective even in 2nd week. Patient will recover. Monitor closely for complications.

complicated.note: Ceftriaxone alone insufficient in perforated typhoid. Emergency surgery is also required.

first_week.note: Good choice for uncomplicated typhoid. Oral azithromycin is effective and suitable. Patient recovers.

second_week.note: Lower efficacy in established disease. IV Ceftriaxone preferred.

complicated.note: Azithromycin alone insufficient for complicated typhoid. Surgery also required.

first_week.note: Outdated. High resistance in South Asia. This isolate is MDR — RESISTANT to chloramphenicol. Bone marrow suppression risk.

second_week.note: Chloramphenicol-resistant strain confirmed. Ineffective and potentially harmful.

complicated.note: Wrong drug for MDR typhoid. Risk of aplastic anaemia.

first_week.note: BLUNDER — giving antimalarial to a patient without malaria. Malaria smear was negative. Delays correct treatment for typhoid.

second_week.note: BLUNDER — malaria excluded. This wastes time and money while typhoid progresses.

complicated.note: FATAL BLUNDER — treating wrong disease while patient deteriorates from typhoid perforation.

first_week.note: Surgery not indicated in uncomplicated typhoid. Unnecessary laparotomy.

second_week.note: Surgery not yet indicated unless perforation confirmed. Manage medically first.

complicated.note: Correct! Emergency laparotomy for typhoid perforation. Perforation repaired, peritoneal washout, drain placed. Continue IV antibiotics post-op. Patient recovering in ICU.

endMessages.perfect: Excellent! You identified enteric fever early, ordered targeted investigations, and started first-line antibiotics promptly. Textbook management.
endMessages.good: Good clinical reasoning. Patient recovered. Minor suboptimal choices — perhaps unnecessary tests or slight treatment delay.
endMessages.average: Patient survived but management had errors. Delayed antibiotics, wrong empirical treatment, or excess investigations.
endMessages.poor: Patient developed serious complications. Typhoid is treatable — it carries significant mortality only when missed or mismanaged. Review this case.
